subject: Chemistry
title: Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle
description: A short lesson to help you apply Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle.

hook.text: You cannot know both the exact position and momentum of an electron at the same time. Nature forbids it.

buildup.text: Werner Heisenberg showed in 1927 that measuring one property precisely makes the other fundamentally uncertain.

discovery.text: This isn't about bad instruments. The uncertainty is built into nature—a particle simply doesn't have both at once.

twist.text: Einstein hated this. He said 'God does not play dice.' Bohr replied: 'Stop telling God what to do.'

climax.text: This principle explains atomic stability. Electrons can't collapse into the nucleus—that would fix their position too precisely.

punchline.text: Nature has a built-in blur. Perfect knowledge is impossible at the atomic scale.

quiz.question: What does Heisenberg's uncertainty principle state?
options: You cannot simultaneously know both the exact position and momentum of a particle
Electrons move too fast to observe accurately
All measurements have errors due to instrument limitations
